result.markdown:
--- title: "Literature Screening & Processing Pipeline" subtitle: "Methodological framework and protocol for mate-choice copying" author: "Eduardo S.A. Santos & Collaborators" format: html: toc: true toc-depth: 3 theme: cosmo self-contained: true execute: echo: true eval: false bibliography: references.bib --- --- ## Overview {#sec-overview} This page documents all data decisions, processing steps, and planned analyses for the systematic review and **meta-analysis of mate-choice copying (MCC) update** across animal taxa. Mate-choice copying occurs when an individual's preference for a potential mate is influenced by observing the mate choices of others — a form of socially acquired preference that has been documented in fish, birds, mammals, and invertebrates. This meta-analysis aims to: 1. Estimate the overall effect size for MCC across species. 2. Quantify between-study heterogeneity. 3. Test for moderating variables (sex, taxon, experimental design). ::: {.callout-note} ## Project status As of this version, the literature screening phase is concluded. This page has been updated with each phase of the pipeline. ::: ### Key numbers at a glance | Metric | Value | |---|---| | Raw records (combined databases) | 2,083 | | Records after deduplication & year filter (≥ 2019) | 495 | | Primary screeners | 15 | | Secondary validators | 2 | | AI-assisted extraction steps | 4 | | Top journals represented | Animal Behaviour, Behavioral Ecology, PRSB | --- ## Data Sources {#sec-data-sources} ### Literature search Records were retrieved from two major bibliographic databases and combined in **Rayyan** format for import into R: - **Scopus** — full-text and abstract search using Boolean terms covering mate choice, copying, non-independent mate selection, and social information use. - **Web of Science (WoS)** — parallel search with identical query structure. The two exports were merged into a single file: `data/0_literature_screening/combined_records_final.csv` ### Master dataset fields ::: {#tbl-fields .cell tbl-cap='Fields in combined_records_final.csv'} ```{.r .cell-code} fields <- tibble::tribble( ~Field, ~Description, ~Completeness, "title", "Article title (primary deduplication key)", "~100%", "doi", "Digital Object Identifier (secondary dedup key)", "~80%", "abstract", "Full abstract text", "~90%", "authors", "Author list", "~95%", "year", "Publication year", "~100%", "journal", "Journal name", "~100%", "source_file", "Which database CSV contributed this record", "100%", "pubmed_id", "PubMed identifier", "Partial", "pmc_id", "PubMed Central identifier", "Partial" ) kable(fields, align = "lll") |> kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed")) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Field Description Completeness
title Article title (primary deduplication key) ~100%
doi Digital Object Identifier (secondary dedup key) ~80%
abstract Full abstract text ~90%
authors Author list ~95%
year Publication year ~100%
journal Journal name ~100%
source_file Which database CSV contributed this record 100%
pubmed_id PubMed identifier Partial
pmc_id PubMed Central identifier Partial
````` ::: ::: ::: {.callout-important} ## Provenance tracking The `source_file` field is preserved through every deduplication step and is concatenated (not lost) when duplicate records from different databases are merged. In the final output it is also appended to the `abstract` field, so that the complete database-of-origin audit trail survives import into Rayyan or any other screening tool. ::: --- ## Processing Pipeline {#sec-pipeline} All deduplication and screener-assignment logic lives in a single reproducible script: > `scripts/0_literature_screening/1_deduplicate_records.R` The script was written in **February 2026** using `data.table` for efficient in-memory processing of the 2,083-record dataset. ### Step 1 — Load data ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} rm(list = ls()) library(dplyr) library(data.table) library(stringr) library(readr) # Rayyan estimated 17,812 records have duplicates across the raw exports articles <- fread("data/0_literature_screening/combined_records_final.csv") ``` ::: ### Step 2 — Title-based deduplication {#sec-title-dedup} Titles are normalised to a canonical lowercase form before matching. When duplicates are found, the **highest-quality** record is retained. **Quality score** (0–3): one point each for having a non-empty abstract, a non-empty author list, and a non-empty DOI. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # --- Normalise title ---------------------------------------------------------- articles[, title_lwr := str_to_lower(title)] articles[, title_lwr := gsub("[[:punct:]]", " ", title_lwr)] # strip punctuation articles[, title_lwr := gsub(" {2,}", " ", title_lwr)] # collapse whitespace articles[, title_lwr := trimws(title_lwr)] # --- Quality score (0–3) ------------------------------------------------------ articles[, has_abstract := ifelse(abstract == "" | is.na(abstract), 0L, 1L)] articles[, has_authors := ifelse(authors == "" | is.na(authors), 0L, 1L)] articles[, has_doi := ifelse(doi == "" | is.na(doi), 0L, 1L)] articles[, quality := has_abstract + has_authors + has_doi] # --- Concatenate source files per title group --------------------------------- sources <- articles[, .(source_file = paste(sort(unique(source_file)), collapse = "; ")), by = .(title_lwr) ] # --- Keep highest-quality record per title ------------------------------------ articles.title.dedupe <- articles[ , .SD[which.max(quality), !c("source_file"), with = FALSE], by = .(title_lwr) ] # --- Merge concatenated sources back in --------------------------------------- articles.title.dedupe.mrg <- merge( articles.title.dedupe, sources, by = "title_lwr", all.x = TRUE ) ``` ::: #### Data decisions — title deduplication | Decision | What was done | Rationale | |---|---|---| | Lowercase conversion | `str_to_lower()` | Catches case differences across databases | | Punctuation removal | `gsub("[[:punct:]]", " ", ...)` | Handles hyphens, apostrophes, colons in titles | | Whitespace collapse | `gsub(" {2,}", " ", ...)` | Normalises after punctuation removal | | Quality scoring | abstract + authors + doi (0/1 each) | Retains the most information-rich duplicate | | Source concatenation | `paste(sort(unique(source_file)), collapse = "; ")` | Full provenance preserved across all duplicates | ### Step 3 — DOI-based deduplication {#sec-doi-dedup} A second pass on the DOI field catches articles in which titles differ slightly between database exports but resolve to the same paper. A custom helper function `split_conc()` uniquely concatenates source strings that were themselves already concatenated in the title-dedup step. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Helper: split already-concatenated source strings, deduplicate, re-join split_conc <- function(x) { str_split(x, pattern = "; ") |> unlist() |> unique() |> sort() |> paste(collapse = "; ") } # Recalculate quality after title dedup (fields may have changed) articles.title.dedupe.mrg[, has_abstract := ifelse(abstract == "" | is.na(abstract), 0L, 1L)] articles.title.dedupe.mrg[, has_authors := ifelse(authors == "" | is.na(authors), 0L, 1L)] articles.title.dedupe.mrg[, has_doi := ifelse(doi == "" | is.na(doi), 0L, 1L)] articles.title.dedupe.mrg[, quality := has_abstract + has_authors + has_doi] # Concatenate sources per DOI sources <- articles.title.dedupe.mrg[, .(source_file = split_conc(source_file)), by = .(doi) ] # Keep highest-quality record per DOI articles.doi.dedupe <- articles.title.dedupe.mrg[ , .SD[which.max(quality), !c("source_file"), with = FALSE], by = .(doi) ] # Merge sources back in articles.final <- merge( articles.doi.dedupe, sources, by = "doi", all.x = TRUE ) ``` ::: ### Step 4 — Clean and append provenance to abstract {#sec-clean} Intermediate processing columns are dropped, and the `source_file` string is appended to the `abstract` field so that provenance survives any downstream tool that does not retain a `source_file` column. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} articles.final[, abstract := paste0(abstract, " | ", source_file)] articles.final <- articles.final[ , !c( "title_lwr", "n_title_matches", "has_abstract", "has_authors", "has_doi", "quality", "n_doi_matches", "source_file" ) ] ``` ::: ### Step 5 — Year filter: 2019 and later {#sec-year-filter} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} articles.final <- articles.final[year >= 2019, ] message("Records after filter: ", nrow(articles.final)) message("Earliest year in dataset: ", min(articles.final$year)) ``` ::: ::: {.callout-important} ## Data decision — year filter Only records published **in or after 2019** are retained for screening. **Rationale:** - Aligns with the pre-registered scope of the review. - Pre-2019 records remain available in `combined_records_final.csv` and can be incorporated in sensitivity analyses. The filter reduces the deduplicated pool to **495 articles**. ::: ### Step 6 — Random shuffle and screener assignment {#sec-assignment} Articles are randomly shuffled before assignment to prevent ordering artefacts (e.g., alphabetical or chronological clustering within screener batches). A dynamic function generates near-equal assignment vectors for any team size. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Team structure screeners <- c( "Aleksandra", "Aneta", "Anna", "Ayumi", "Cassidy", "Christine", "Erick", "Iwo", "Hao", "Jimuel", "Kyle", "Marija", "Santiago", "Sergio", "Shinichi", # 15 primary screeners "Losia", "Eduardo" # 2 secondary validators ) primary_list <- setdiff(screeners, c("Eduardo", "Losia")) secondary_list <- c("Eduardo", "Losia") # Random shuffle (prevents date/alpha ordering bias) set.seed(NULL) # no fixed seed — shuffled output files are the reproducible record articles.final <- articles.final[sample(.N), ] # Dynamic assignment: ceiling(total / n) reps, trimmed to total rows create_assignments <- function(names, total_rows) { reps_needed <- ceiling(total_rows / length(names)) full_vector <- rep(names, each = reps_needed) return(full_vector[seq_len(total_rows)]) } n_total <- nrow(articles.final) # Primary: ~33 articles per screener (495 / 15) articles.final[, primary_screener := create_assignments(primary_list, n_total)] # Secondary: ~248 articles per validator (495 / 2) articles.final[, secondary_screener := create_assignments(secondary_list, n_total)] # Verify balance articles.final[, .(n = .N), by = .(primary_screener)] articles.final[, .(n = .N), by = .(secondary_screener)] ``` ::: ### Step 7 — Export per-screener CSVs {#sec-export} One CSV is written per primary screener to `builds/0_literature_screening/deduplicated_and_randomly_split/`. Each file retains both assignment columns for cross-referencing. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} invisible(lapply( unique(articles.final$primary_screener), function(x) { write_csv( articles.final[primary_screener == x, ], paste0("builds/0_literature_screening/deduplicated_and_randomly_split/", x, ".csv") ) } )) ``` ::: --- ## Literature Screening {#sec-screening} ### Team structure The 495 articles are distributed across a 17-person team. Each article receives: - **One primary screen** — eligibility assessment against pre-registered inclusion criteria. - **One independent secondary validation** — every article is also assigned to one of two validators (Eduardo or Losia), enabling inter-rater reliability estimation. ::: {#tbl-team .cell tbl-cap='Screener assignments'} ```{.r .cell-code} team <- tibble::tribble( ~Screener, ~Role, ~Articles, "Aleksandra", "Primary", "~33", "Aneta", "Primary", "~33", "Anna", "Primary", "~33", "Ayumi", "Primary", "~33", "Cassidy", "Primary", "~33", "Christine", "Primary", "~33", "Erick", "Primary", "~33", "Hao", "Primary", "~33", "Iwo", "Primary", "~33", "Jimuel", "Primary", "~33", "Kyle", "Primary", "~33", "Marija", "Primary", "~33", "Santiago", "Primary", "~33", "Sergio", "Primary", "~33", "Shinichi", "Primary", "~33", "Eduardo", "Validator", "~248", "Losia", "Validator", "~248" ) kable(team, align = "lll") |> kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed")) |> row_spec(16:17, bold = TRUE, background = "#fdf4e3") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Screener Role Articles
Aleksandra Primary ~33
Aneta Primary ~33
Anna Primary ~33
Ayumi Primary ~33
Cassidy Primary ~33
Christine Primary ~33
Erick Primary ~33
Hao Primary ~33
Iwo Primary ~33
Jimuel Primary ~33
Kyle Primary ~33
Marija Primary ~33
Santiago Primary ~33
Sergio Primary ~33
Shinichi Primary ~33
Eduardo Validator ~248
Losia Validator ~248
````` ::: ::: ### Inclusion criteria Articles are included if they: 1. Report an **experimental** test of mate-choice copying (observer watches a demonstrator make a mate choice, then has the opportunity to choose between the same mates). 2. Provide sufficient quantitative data for effect-size extraction (means + variance, or counts, or test statistics with sample sizes). 3. Were published **2019 or later** (pre-registration scope). 4. Are written in English. ### Inter-rater reliability Disagreements between primary and secondary screeners will be resolved by discussion or a designated tie-breaker. --- ## Data Extraction Pipeline {#sec-extraction} Included articles are sent to **Google NotebookLM** for AI-assisted data extraction via a four-step structured prompting protocol. All prompts are grounded by a `MetaAnalysis_Reference_Sheet.pdf` data dictionary containing few-shot examples of correct extractions. ::: {.callout-warning} ## Cache-bust rule Each step must be run in a **completely new** NotebookLM chat window. Re-using a previous window causes the model to recycle earlier tables rather than reading the PDFs afresh. ::: ### Step 1 — Species & study identification **Goal:** Establish taxonomic accuracy across all included papers. Each PDF yields one row in a Markdown table with two columns: | Column | Format | Example | |---|---|---| | `Study_ID` | `FirstAuthorLastName_Year_CamelCaseJournal` | `Belkina_2021_AnimalBehaviour` | | `Species` | Full binomial nomenclature | *Poecilia reticulata* | Common names alone are not accepted. The Study ID format is consistent across all four steps to serve as the primary join key. ### Step 2 — Co-authorship & lab identification **Goal:** Build a bipartite co-authorship network to identify "hub labs." Output is in **long format** — one row per author per paper — so that network edges can be constructed directly. | Column | Description | |---|---| | `Study_ID` | Join key | | `Author_Name` | Full name | | `Author_Position` | First / Last / Corresponding / Middle | | `Institutional_Affiliation` | University or institute | | `Lab_Identifier` | `PILastName_Institution_City` (e.g., `Dukas_UniversityOfToronto_Toronto`) | ::: {.callout-note} ## Lab identifier design The **last author** is designated as the lab PI, and the same `Lab_Identifier` string is applied to every co-author row on the same paper. This links all authors to one "primary lab" node in the bipartite network, enabling correct degree-centrality calculation for hub-lab detection. ::: ### Step 3 — Trial-level data mapping **Goal:** Map the internal experimental structure of each paper — how many distinct trials exist, where their data live, and whether subjects are independent. | Column | Description | |---|---| | `Study_ID` | Join key | | `Trial_ID` | Experiment name/number (e.g., "Experiment 1", "Treatment A") | | `Trial_Description` | 1–2 sentence summary of manipulation | | `Sample_Size_N` | Focal subjects in this trial | | `Subject_Independence` | "Independent Cohort" or "Repeated Measures" | | `Control_Group_Shared` | Yes/No + which trial (if Yes) | | `Data_Location` | Exact page and figure/table reference (e.g., "Table 1, Page 5") | ### Step 4 — Quantitative extraction (multi-agent pipeline) **Goal:** Extract and cross-validate the actual numbers using a simulated three-agent architecture. #### Agent architecture We utilize a three-agent quantitative extraction pipeline: * **Agent 1 (Extractor):** Pulls raw statistical data (means, variances, counts, and test statistics) directly from the Methods and Results text and tables. * **Agent 2 (Verifier):** Cross-checks calculations, verifies sample independence, identifies any SD vs. SE confusion, and scans the text to ensure no secondary trials were missed. * **Agent 3 (Arbiter):** Resolves any discrepancies between Agent 1 and Agent 2, standardizes reporting, and outputs the final clean data row. **Agent 2 checks explicitly for three systematic extraction errors:** 1. **SD/SE confusion** — a common error in manual extraction that biases heterogeneity estimates if uncorrected. 2. **Sample independence** — flags whether the N used is independent across trials or from repeated measures on the same subjects. 3. **Missing trials** — re-reads the Methods to confirm no secondary experiments were missed by Agent 1. #### Final output columns | Column | Content | |---|---| | `Study_ID` | Author_Year_JournalName | | `Trial_ID` | Specific experiment or condition | | `Response_Metric` | What was measured | | `Validated_Exp_Group_Data` | Mean ± SD/SE, Counts, or Test Stat | | `Validated_Control_Group_Data` | Mean ± SD/SE, Counts, or NA | | `SD_or_SE` | Explicit variance metric label | | `Agent1_Initial_Pull` | Brief summary of original extraction | | `Agent2_Verification_Notes` | Corrections made, or "No missing trials found" | | `Verification_Location` | Exact page and table/figure number | --- ## Effect Size Calculation {#sec-effect-sizes} Following data extraction, effect sizes and their sampling variances are calculated using the reproducible Python pipeline located at [scripts/1_effect_size_calculation_pipeline/calculate_effect_sizes.py](file:///Users/eduardosantos/Documents/Repos/mate_choice_meta/scripts/1_effect_size_calculation_pipeline/calculate_effect_sizes.py). The raw data is stored in the Excel spreadsheet [data/1_effect_size_calculation_pipeline/Data Extraction Mate Choice Meta Analysis.xlsx](file:///Users/eduardosantos/Documents/Repos/mate_choice_meta/data/1_effect_size_calculation_pipeline/Data%20Extraction%20Mate%20Choice%20Meta%20Analysis.xlsx) inside the `EduardoExtraction` sheet. To support tracking, each row contains an `identifierEffectSizeID` column containing a unique sequential identifier for each control–experimental comparison (e.g., `es0001`, `es0002`). The Python pipeline dynamically resolves column locations by scanning the header row, computes the following effect size metrics, and saves them back to the spreadsheet: ### 1. Hedges' $g$ (Bias-Corrected Standardised Mean Difference) Calculated depending on the data type extracted: * **From Means & SDs:** Using control and treatment means, standard deviations (or standard errors converted using $SD = SE \times \sqrt{n}$), and sample sizes. * **From $t$-statistics:** Supported for both paired/one-sample designs (Case 2, in the Python script) and independent-sample designs (Case 3, assuming an equal sample split if individual group sizes are unknown). ### 2. Odds Ratio (OR) Calculated for categorical outcomes from $2 \times 2$ contingency tables ($A$, $B$, $C$, $D$), where the sampling variance of the log odds ratio is approximated using the delta method. ### 3. Odds Ratio $\leftrightarrow$ Hedges' $g$ Cross-Conversion For rows containing only one of the two metrics, the missing metric is derived using the logistic-normal approximation [@chinnSimpleMethodConverting2000; @borensteinIntroductionMetaanalysis2011]: * **$\text{OR} \rightarrow \text{Hedges' } g$**: $d = \ln(\text{OR}) \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\pi}$ and then bias-corrected to $g$ using the $J$ correction factor. * **$\text{Hedges' } g \rightarrow \text{OR}$**: $\ln(\text{OR}) = g \times \frac{\pi}{\sqrt{3}}$ and $\text{Var}(\ln\text{OR}) = \text{Var}(g) \times \frac{\pi^2}{3}$. To execute the calculations and update the workbook, run: ```bash python scripts/1_effect_size_calculation_pipeline/calculate_effect_sizes.py ``` --- ## Planned Analyses {#sec-analyses} All models will be implemented in R. Our pre-registration details are available in Santos et al. [-@santosMatechoiceCopyingNonhuman2026]. ### Meta-analytic models Multi-level random-effects meta-analysis to estimate the overall effect size for MCC, accounting for trials nested within papers nested within labs. Package: `metafor`. **Heterogeneity** will be quantified with $I^2$ and $\tau^2$ at each level. ### Phylogenetic comparative methods Species non-independence is controlled via a phylogenetic covariance matrix incorporated into the meta-analytic model (`metafor` + `ape`). This accounts for the possibility that closely related species share similar MCC propensity due to shared evolutionary history. ### Publication bias Funnel plot asymmetry, and Egger's regression test to assess publication bias. Time-lag bias will be examined by plotting effect size against publication year. ### Moderator analyses Meta-regression models will test whether effect sizes vary by: - Sex of the copying individual (female vs. male) - Taxonomic group (fish, bird, mammal, invertebrate) - Experimental design (before-and-after vs. no pre-test) - Copying mechanism (individual vs. generalized) ### Sensitivity analyses - Inclusion of pre-2019 records. - Leave-one-out analysis for influential studies.
